title: How the Closest Planet to the Sun Lost Its Air

lesson.learn: Mercury cannot hold a thick atmosphere because it is small, hot, and constantly blasted by the solar wind. Its gravity is weak, so gas molecules escape easily. Instead of a stable atmosphere, Mercury has a thin exosphere that is continually replenished by surface atoms knocked loose by sunlight, micrometeorites, and charged particles.
lesson.simpler: Mercury is too small and too close to the Sun to keep its air. Any gas that reaches the planet is quickly stripped away or escapes into space.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Mercury lacks a true atmosphere because heat, weak gravity, and solar wind all work together to remove gases faster than the planet can keep them.

learnImageCredit.creator: Age234 at the English Wikipedia project

lesson.deeperDive: Mercury's atmosphere problem is better described as a constant loss-and-replacement cycle than a single event. The planet's exosphere is made of atoms that rarely collide, so it behaves very differently from the dense atmospheres on Earth or Venus. Spacecraft data, especially from MESSENGER, showed that Mercury's surface is being continually modified by solar wind sputtering, photon-stimulated desorption, and micrometeorite impacts. These processes release sodium, potassium, calcium, and other elements into the exosphere, but the planet cannot retain them for long. Mercury's weak magnetic field offers only limited shielding, so the Sun keeps stripping the planet's thin envelope away.
lesson.funFact: An atom in Mercury's exosphere can travel a long distance without ever colliding with another particle.
references: Mercury exosphere and solar wind interaction research

places: Mercury's orbit close to the Sun
NASA and ESA planetary science centers
years: 1990s - Mercury's weak field and environment are studied in detail
